text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Genitourinary Oncology Service

PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
DOB: (age 63)
Diagnosis: Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), right kidney, with bilateral pulmonary metastases; sarcomatoid differentiation 20%; pT3b pN1 M1
ECOG PS: 1
Visit Type: Restaging / Treatment Response Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo presents for her scheduled first formal restaging assessment approximately 3 months after initiation of first-line systemic immunotherapy. She completed 4 cycles of combination ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate 214 regimen) and has now transitioned to nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance. She presents today to review restaging CT imaging and assess overall treatment response, tolerability, and ongoing management plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old Nigerian-American woman with a diagnosis of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney, notable for high-risk histologic features including Fuhrman nuclear grade 4 and sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the tumor volume. Her initial presentation included a 14.2 cm right renal mass with macroscopic tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein (T3b), a single positive right retroperitoneal hilar lymph node (2.4 cm, pN1), and bilateral pulmonary nodules on CT (M1). MET amplification was identified on comprehensive tumor NGS profiling (Foundation One CDx equivalent). She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 3.5 months ago (R0 resection), with postoperative course notable for Grade 2 ileus (resolved) and mild anemia managed with oral iron. Her IMDC risk classification is intermediate risk (2 factors: hemoglobin below LLN, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than 1 year).

Six weeks following nephrectomy, she initiated first-line systemic therapy per the CheckMate 214 regimen (ipilimumab + nivolumab) given intermediate IMDC risk and, critically, the presence of sarcomatoid differentiation - a pre-specified subgroup in CheckMate 214 in which dual checkpoint inhibition demonstrated particularly compelling benefit. PD-L1 CPS was 12 on her pre-operative biopsy specimen.

Her course on combination immunotherapy was complicated by Grade 2 immune-related colitis occurring after cycle 2, characterized by 5-7 loose watery stools per day. This was managed with oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with a prolonged 8-week taper. Colonoscopy confirmed diffuse pan-colitis without ulceration; infectious etiologies including C. difficile and CMV were excluded. Per protocol, ipilimumab was permanently discontinued given Grade 2 irAE; nivolumab was held during the active steroid course and resumed upon completion of the taper after shared decision-making with the patient. She completed the remaining 4 cycles of the induction phase with nivolumab as a single agent (ipilimumab not reintroduced), and subsequently transitioned to flat-dose nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks for maintenance approximately 3 to 4 weeks ago.

Since the steroid taper was completed approximately 2-3 weeks ago, the patient reports gradual improvement in her energy level and overall sense of well-being. She denies any recurrence of diarrhea or abdominal cramping. She notes some baseline fatigue but describes it as mild and manageable. She has resumed light activities at home, including some household tasks and brief walks in her neighborhood. She is a retired nurse who lives with her adult daughter, who attends clinic with her today.

A notable new finding on laboratory assessment today: thyroid function tests show subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 7.8 mIU/L, free T4 low-normal), most consistent with immune-related hypothyroidism - a recognized class effect of PD-1 inhibitor therapy. This is being addressed today (see plan).

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast was performed prior to today's visit. Imaging demonstrates a mixed response: the dominant right lower lobe (RLL) target lesion has increased in size from 1.8 cm at baseline to 2.1 cm (+17%), raising the differential of pseudoprogression versus true progression in the immunotherapy context. Conversely, both remaining target lesions (RLL second nodule, LLL nodule) have decreased in size (0.7 cm and 0.5 cm, respectively). The sum of target lesion diameters is 3.3 cm compared to 3.4 cm at baseline, reflecting overall stable disease per RECIST 1.1 with a net change of -3%. No new extrapulmonary disease was identified.

Given the heterogeneous behavior of the pulmonary metastases - one lesion enlarging while others are regressing - the clinical question of pseudoprogression versus true progression of target lesion 1 is foremost in today's assessment. The overall RECIST categorization remains stable disease. The plan is to continue nivolumab and perform confirmatory repeat imaging in 6 weeks rather than prematurely discontinuing potentially effective immunotherapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, improving over the past 2 weeks since steroid taper completion. Appetite improved. No fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss.
Gastrointestinal: No diarrhea, no abdominal cramping, no hematochezia, no melena. Bowel movements now formed and regular (1 per day). Good appetite. No nausea or vomiting.
Respiratory: Mild intermittent exertional dyspnea with brisk activity; no dyspnea at rest. No cough, no hemoptysis, no pleuritic chest pain.
Genitourinary: Nocturia x1-2 (chronic/baseline). No dysuria. No hematuria. No flank pain.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. No arthralgias (prior immune-related arthralgias were mild and have since resolved during steroid course). No myalgias.
Neurological: No headaches, no visual changes, no focal weakness or sensory deficits. No gait disturbance.
Skin: No rash currently. No pruritus. No new skin lesions.
Endocrine: Reports mild cold intolerance over the past 4-6 weeks and mild constipation tendency (consistent with hypothyroidism). No polyuria or polydipsia.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
Psychiatric: Reports some anxiety about scan results. No depression per PHQ-2 screen today (score 1). Sleeping well.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, well-appearing, conversational woman in no acute distress. Accompanied by adult daughter. Appropriately groomed and engaged.
Vital Signs: BP 126/80 mmHg (left arm), HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Temperature 36.8 C, Weight 78.2 kg (stable from prior visit). BMI 28.9.
HEENT: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Thyroid: No palpable thyromegaly. No nodules appreciated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or decreased breath sounds. Good air movement throughout.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Well-healed right flank/subcostal surgical incision (approximately 18 cm, healing well, no erythema or drainage). No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. No rebound or guarding. Non-tender throughout.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds.
Skin: No rash, no vitiligo, no erythema, no lesions. No palmar or plantar changes.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal.
Lymph nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy (cervical, axillary, inguinal).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 6.4 x10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC: 4.1 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, mild; improved from 9.2 g/dL post-operatively)
  - MCV: 84 fL (normocytic)
  - Platelets: 224 x10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 46 mL/min/1.73m2, consistent with expected post-nephrectomy baseline, solitary left kidney)
  - Glucose: 108 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with type 2 diabetes - fasting not confirmed)
  - Calcium (corrected): 9.6 mg/dL (normal)
  - ALT: 24 U/L, AST: 28 U/L, Alkaline Phosphatase: 82 U/L, Total Bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (all within normal limits; no immune-related hepatitis)
  - Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
  - LDH: 204 U/L (mildly elevated above ULN of 190 U/L - mildly so, noted)

Endocrine:
  - TSH: 7.8 mIU/L (elevated; upper limit of normal 4.5 mIU/L) -- NEW finding today
  - Free T4: 0.82 ng/dL (low-normal; normal range 0.8-1.8 ng/dL)
  - Anti-TPO antibody: pending at time of note

Urinalysis: Protein trace (dipstick), no casts, no hematuria. Spot urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.18 (within normal limits).

HbA1c: 7.1% (reasonable glycemic control; no change in metformin dosing needed at this time).

Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio: 2.8 (stable).

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed today)

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (restaging, first on-treatment scan):

Status post right radical nephrectomy. No local recurrence identified at the right nephrectomy bed. No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (previously resected hilar node site unremarkable).

Pulmonary metastases - RECIST 1.1 target lesions:
  - Target Lesion 1: Right lower lobe dominant nodule -- 2.1 cm (increased from 1.8 cm at baseline, +17%). This lesion demonstrates slight internal heterogeneity; differential includes pseudoprogression in the setting of active immunotherapy versus true progression.
  - Target Lesion 2: Right lower lobe second nodule -- 0.7 cm (decreased from 0.9 cm at baseline, -22%). Favorable response.
  - Target Lesion 3: Left lower lobe nodule -- 0.5 cm (decreased from 0.7 cm at baseline, -29%). Favorable response.
  - Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.3 cm (from 3.4 cm at baseline; net change -3%, RECIST 1.1: Stable Disease).
  - Non-target lesions: One previously subcentimeter nodule has increased from <5 mm to 0.5 cm - remains non-target per RECIST 1.1 criteria.

No new pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pericardial effusion. No evidence of pneumonitis (specifically assessed given immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy).

Abdomen/pelvis: No hepatic lesions. No adrenal metastases. No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No ascites. Post-surgical changes in right renal fossa only.

Overall RECIST 1.1 Assessment: Stable disease. The divergent behavior of target lesion 1 (enlarging) versus target lesions 2 and 3 (shrinking) represents a mixed response pattern. In the context of PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade, the enlargement of target lesion 1 may represent pseudoprogression (tumor infiltration by immune cells with resultant transient enlargement) rather than true disease progression. Confirmatory imaging in 6 weeks is planned.

---

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (Fuhrman grade 4, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, pT3b pN1 M1) who is approximately 3 months into first-line immunotherapy. She completed 4 cycles of combination ipilimumab/nivolumab (CheckMate 214 regimen), with ipilimumab permanently discontinued after Grade 2 immune-related colitis, and has transitioned to nivolumab monotherapy maintenance (flat dose 480 mg IV q4 weeks).

1. Stage IV ccRCC - mixed/stable response on first restaging CT. The overall RECIST assessment is stable disease with a net -3% change in the sum of target lesion diameters. The key clinical challenge is the divergent behavior of the pulmonary lesions: target lesion 1 (RLL dominant, 1.8 -> 2.1 cm, +17%) has grown, while target lesions 2 and 3 have clearly regressed. This pattern is not consistent with unequivocal progression and raises genuine concern for pseudoprogression, a recognized phenomenon with PD-1/CTLA-4 therapy that may occur in approximately 5-10% of patients and is more common in patients receiving combination IO. Pseudoprogression can reflect immune cell infiltration and early tumor inflammation rather than net tumor growth. The absence of new lesions, new extrapulmonary sites, or clinical deterioration supports a watch-and-wait approach rather than premature treatment discontinuation.

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism (new) - TSH 7.8 mIU/L with low-normal free T4. This is a well-recognized irAE associated with anti-PD-1 therapy, occurring in 5-10% of patients on nivolumab, and is more common following combination ipi/nivo. The patient endorses mild symptoms consistent with hypothyroidism (cold intolerance, constipation). Levothyroxine replacement is indicated.

3. Prior Grade 2 immune-related colitis - resolved. Colonoscopy-confirmed immune-mediated pan-colitis, managed with prolonged prednisone taper. Now fully resolved (Grade 0). No evidence of recurrence. Ipilimumab permanently discontinued; nivolumab resumed.

4. Mild normocytic anemia, Grade 1 (Hgb 10.8 g/dL) - improving since post-operative period (from 9.2 g/dL). Likely multifactorial: chronic disease/inflammation, chronic kidney disease (eGFR ~46), and prior post-operative blood loss. No transfusion required.

5. Subclinical renal insufficiency (eGFR ~46 mL/min/1.73m2) - stable, expected in the setting of solitary kidney. Metformin continued with ongoing renal function monitoring; dose currently appropriate for this eGFR. No nephrotoxic agents added.

6. Diabetes mellitus type 2 - HbA1c 7.1%, well-controlled on metformin. No dose adjustment needed.

7. Hypertension - BP well-controlled today (126/80 mmHg) on amlodipine 5 mg daily. No changes needed.

---

PLAN

1. Stage IV ccRCC / Mixed response on first restaging:
   - Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (flat dose maintenance). The mixed response pattern with RECIST stable disease and the temporal context of active PD-1 blockade favor continuation rather than therapy switch. True progression cannot be confirmed in the absence of new lesions and with overall stable disease sum of diameters.
   - Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in 6 weeks (confirmatory scan specifically to evaluate target lesion 1 and to rule out emergence of new disease sites). If target lesion 1 continues to grow or new lesions emerge on this confirmatory scan, this will confirm true progression and prompt discussion of second-line therapy options (cabozantinib or lenvatinib/everolimus).
   - Continue to monitor for all irAEs at each nivolumab infusion visit (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, glucose, and symptoms review prior to each cycle).
   - MET amplification noted on tumor NGS; this remains a potentially actionable finding for second-line therapy (cabozantinib, a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor, would be a particularly attractive choice in the second-line setting given this molecular context).

2. Immune-related hypothyroidism:
   - Initiate levothyroxine 50 mcg orally daily. Counsel patient to take on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before first meal or coffee.
   - Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks to assess response and titrate dose as needed.
   - No need to hold nivolumab for Grade 1-2 immune-related endocrinopathy managed with hormone replacement per NCCN and ASCO irAE management guidelines.
   - Counsel patient on signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism versus hyperthyroidism (rare thyroiditis flare).

3. Prior immune-related colitis (resolved):
   - No further prednisone required. Monitor for recurrence of GI symptoms. Patient instructed to contact oncology nurse immediately if diarrhea recurs, especially with nivolumab continuation.
   - Ipilimumab permanently discontinued (will not be reintroduced). Documented allergy/irAE.

4. Anemia:
   - Mild Grade 1, stable trend toward improvement. Continue monitoring on serial CBCs. Continue oral iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily with vitamin C).
   - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not indicated at this time. Transfusion threshold not reached.

5. Renal function:
   - Monitor creatinine and eGFR every 4-6 weeks. Maintain adequate hydration.
   - Hold metformin if eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m2; currently safe to continue at eGFR ~46.
   - Avoid nephrotoxic medications and contrast use with appropriate hydration/premedication protocols.

6. Diabetes and hypertension:
   - Continue metformin as above. Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily (blood pressure at goal today).
   - Coordinate ongoing management with primary care physician.

7. Supportive care:
   - Fatigue: Patient counseled on energy conservation strategies, graded aerobic activity (walking program as tolerated). Fatigue anticipated to improve further as immune-related events resolve and as systemic inflammation diminishes.
   - Nutritional support: Referred to oncology dietitian for protein-adequate, anti-inflammatory diet counseling.
   - Psychosocial: Brief anxiety screening today (PHQ-2 score 1, not meeting threshold for major depressive disorder). Will monitor. Social work referral offered; patient declines at this time but acknowledges support from her daughter.

8. Return to clinic:
   - Next medical oncology visit in 6 weeks, coinciding with next restaging CT (confirmatory scan for target lesion 1 assessment) and next nivolumab infusion.
   - Patient instructed to call with any new or worsening symptoms between visits: fever, diarrhea (>4 stools/day), shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, new neurological symptoms, or severe fatigue.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 45 minutes.

Attending Medical Oncologist
Genitourinary Oncology Service
Academic Medical Center
